title: Module 3: Complex & Double-Sided Reflections - Exploring Ambivalence
learning_objective: Learn to use complex reflections to capture deeper meaning and double-sided reflections to acknowledge ambivalence without pushing for resolution
scenario_context: Complex reflections add meaning, emotion, or deeper understanding beyond what the patient explicitly said. Double-sided reflections acknowledge both sides of ambivalence (reasons to change AND reasons to stay the same), helping patients hold their complexity without feeling pushed.

persona_config.name: But
persona_config.background: Recognizes there is a problem and is considering making changes but has not yet committed.

node_1.persona_text: I've been thinking about quitting smoking a lot lately. My daughter's pregnant with my first grandchild, and she asked me not to smoke around the baby. But I've been smoking for 25 years - it's part of who I am.

choice_points.option_text: Your daughter's pregnancy is making you think about quitting.

feedback.immediate: This is accurate but only reflects one side - the reason to change. In contemplation, double-sided reflections that honor both sides of ambivalence are often more helpful.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: On one hand, becoming a grandparent is making you think about quitting. On the other hand, smoking has been part of your life for so long, it feels like part of your identity.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This double-sided reflection acknowledges both sides of their ambivalence without trying to resolve it. This helps them feel fully heard and understood.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Smoking has become part of your identity over 25 years.

feedback.immediate: This reflects only the sustain talk side (reasons not to change). While sometimes useful strategically, you missed the change talk about the grandchild. A double-sided reflection would be more complete here.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_2a.persona_text: Yeah, but it's not just that. I mean, I don't even know who I'd be without cigarettes. They're how I take breaks at work, how I deal with stress, how I... I don't know, punctuate my day.

choice_points.option_text: So you're thinking about quitting for your grandchild, and you're also aware that smoking serves a lot of important functions in your daily life.

feedback.immediate: Good recovery! This double-sided reflection brings back the change talk you missed initially while also honoring what they're emphasizing now. This balances the conversation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Cigarettes structure your whole day - breaks, stress relief, transitions.

feedback.immediate: This reflection accurately captures what they said, but continuing to reflect only sustain talk can reinforce reasons not to change. Consider bringing back both sides of the ambivalence.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_2b.persona_text: Exactly! You get it. I want to be a good grandparent, but I also don't want to lose myself. And honestly, I'm scared. What if I quit and I'm miserable? What if I fail again like I did five years ago?

choice_points.option_text: There's a lot at stake here - being there for your grandchild, maintaining your sense of self, and the risk of another painful failure.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection synthesizes multiple elements they've shared - competing values and fears. It shows deep understanding of their complex situation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You want to be a good grandparent and you're afraid of failing again.

feedback.immediate: This is accurate but simpler than what the moment calls for. They've shared a complex emotional landscape - a complex reflection would show deeper understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: It sounds like you're scared of both changing and not changing.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection names the paradox of their ambivalence - fear in both directions. This kind of reflection can be very powerful in contemplation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_2c.persona_text: Right. Everyone keeps telling me to quit, but they don't understand what it means to me. It's not just a habit - it's... it's mine, you know?

choice_points.option_text: People telling you to quit makes you feel like they don't understand how significant smoking is to you. And at the same time, your daughter's request about the baby is coming from a place that also matters to you.

feedback.immediate: Excellent recovery! This acknowledges their frustration while gently bringing back the change talk side. You're helping them hold both sides rather than getting stuck defending one side.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: People don't understand what smoking means to you.

feedback.immediate: This validates their feeling but keeps them stuck on the sustain talk side. In contemplation, we want to help them explore both sides of their ambivalence.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

node_3a.persona_text: Yeah, that's it exactly. I'm pulled in both directions. When I think about holding my grandchild, I want to be smoke-free. But when I think about getting through a tough day at work without a cigarette break... I can't even imagine it.

choice_points.option_text: In one moment, the image of holding your grandchild makes quitting feel important and necessary. In another moment, facing work stress makes smoking feel absolutely essential.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This double-sided reflection shows how their feelings about smoking shift depending on the context they're imagining. This validates that ambivalence is complex and situational.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You can see yourself wanting different things depending on the situation.

feedback.immediate: This is accurate but less specific than the moment calls for. The previous option captures the specific situations and emotions they described, showing deeper listening.
feedback.learning_note: Active listening through reflection builds trust and therapeutic alliance.

node_3b.persona_text: Right. And my coworkers all smoke - that's our social time. If I quit, I'm sitting in my cubicle alone while they're all outside talking and laughing. I'd be isolated.

choice_points.option_text: So there's your grandchild on one side, and on the other side there's this whole network of important things - stress relief, work breaks, social connection with coworkers. You're weighing a lot here.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This brings back the change talk (grandchild) while also fully acknowledging all the sustain talk they've shared. The phrase 'you're weighing a lot' normalizes the difficulty of their position.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Quitting would mean losing that social connection with your coworkers.

feedback.immediate: While accurate, this continues to reflect only one side. The conversation is at risk of reinforcing reasons not to change. Bring back both sides with a double-sided reflection.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_3c.persona_text: Yes! That's exactly it. It's not a simple decision. There's so much wrapped up in this. And I feel like everyone wants me to just decide already, but I can't.

choice_points.option_text: People wanting you to decide quickly doesn't honor how complex this really is for you.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection captures the deeper meaning - they need their complexity honored, not rushed. This shows you understand what they really need.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You're not ready to decide yet.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but doesn't capture why they're not ready - the complexity and the external pressure. The previous option shows deeper understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: Part of you wants clarity and another part knows you need time to sit with this complexity.

feedback.immediate: Good! This reflects the internal tension between wanting resolution and needing time. Both this and the previous complex reflection are excellent - they capture different aspects of the situation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_3d.persona_text: I am. Five years ago I tried to quit cold turkey. I made it three weeks and then I cracked. I was so irritable, my family couldn't stand me. I felt like a failure.

choice_points.option_text: That experience - making it three weeks but then struggling and feeling like you'd failed - that memory makes the idea of trying again even scarier.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection connects their past experience to their current fear, showing you understand how the past is shaping their present hesitation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You tried to quit five years ago and it didn't work out.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but doesn't capture the emotional weight of that experience - the irritability, family tension, and sense of failure. A complex reflection would show deeper empathy.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_3e.persona_text: That's... yeah. That's exactly it. If I keep smoking, I'm scared I'll disappoint my daughter and not be the grandparent I want to be. But if I try to quit, I'm scared I'll fail and feel terrible about myself.

choice_points.option_text: You're caught between two different kinds of disappointment - disappointing your daughter or disappointing yourself.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection names the painful paradox - both paths involve potential disappointment. This helps them see their dilemma clearly.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Either way, there's fear.

feedback.immediate: True but quite general. The previous option is more specific about the types of fear and disappointment in each direction, which shows deeper understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3f.persona_text: It does. I mean, I know she's worried about the baby. I get that. But I also need people to respect that this is hard for me. It's not like I'm not trying to be considerate.

choice_points.option_text: You understand where your daughter is coming from, and you also need your own struggle to be acknowledged and respected.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This double-sided reflection honors both their empathy for their daughter and their need for understanding. This is the essence of holding ambivalence.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You need people to see how hard this is for you.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but only captures one side - their need for understanding. You missed the important acknowledgment of their daughter's perspective.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_3g.persona_text: They don't. They just see a bad habit. They don't see that when my mom died, cigarettes got me through. They don't see that it's how I celebrate good things too. It's woven into everything.

choice_points.option_text: Smoking has been there for you through the hardest times and the best times - it's connected to your emotional life in profound ways.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection captures the deep emotional significance - smoking isn't just a habit but is intertwined with their emotional experiences and coping.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You want to honor your grandchild and you also need to honor what smoking has meant to you through grief and celebration.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This double-sided reflection frames both sides in terms of honoring - honoring the grandchild and honoring their own emotional history. This shows deep respect for their complexity.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4a.persona_text: Exactly. And I hate feeling this torn. I wish I could just decide and be done with it. But every time I lean one way, I immediately think of reasons to go the other way.

choice_points.option_text: This back and forth is exhausting. Part of you wishes for clarity, and another part of you isn't quite ready to let go of either side.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This captures both the exhaustion and why they can't resolve it yet - they're not ready to let go of either side. This validates their experience.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

choice_points.option_text: The ambivalence is wearing you down.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but doesn't capture the complexity they just described - the constant back and forth and the wish for resolution. The previous option shows deeper understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4b.persona_text: I am weighing a lot. And honestly? The grandchild is the biggest thing that's ever made me seriously consider quitting. But I still don't know if that's enough to outweigh everything else.

choice_points.option_text: Your grandchild represents the strongest reason you've ever had to quit, and you're still not sure if even that tips the scales.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection amplifies both the significance of this motivator (strongest ever) and the depth of their ambivalence (even that might not be enough). This honors where they are.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: The grandchild makes you want to quit more than anything else has.

feedback.immediate: This reflects the change talk but misses the important second part - their uncertainty about whether even this is enough. The complexity they're expressing deserves a more complex reflection.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4c.persona_text: Right. And it's not just work. My best friend and I have been smoking together for 20 years. Our whole friendship is built around those conversations over cigarettes. What happens to that?

choice_points.option_text: So you're imagining the baby - your first grandchild - and all the reasons that makes quitting feel important. And you're also imagining what you might lose - work friendships, your 20-year friendship, those important conversations. These are real relationships at stake on both sides.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This brings back the change talk that's been missing while fully honoring all the relationships they'd worry about losing. It frames both sides in terms of relationships, which seems to be what matters most to them.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

choice_points.option_text: You're worried about losing that 20-year friendship.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but the conversation has become one-sided - only exploring reasons not to change. A double-sided reflection would help bring back balance.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4d.persona_text: Thank you for getting that. Most people just tell me to make a pros and cons list, like it's that simple. But it's not about logic - it's about... my whole life.

choice_points.option_text: This isn't a simple math equation you can solve with a list - it's about your identity, your relationships, your emotional life. The stakes feel much bigger than pros and cons.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection names why this decision is so difficult - it touches on core aspects of their life. This shows you understand the depth of what they're facing.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: This is about more than logic for you.

feedback.immediate: True but doesn't capture what makes it more than logic - the identity, relationships, and emotional dimensions they've shared. The previous option shows much deeper understanding.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4e.persona_text: Yeah. I remember how it felt to fail. And thinking about going through that again... it's almost worse than just accepting I'm a smoker forever.

choice_points.option_text: The fear of another failure feels more powerful right now than the pull to change for your grandchild.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection names what they're experiencing - fear is currently stronger than desire. This doesn't judge or try to change it, just reflects it accurately.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You're scared of failing again.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but doesn't capture the comparison they made - that this fear might be stronger than the desire to change. The complexity of that deserves a complex reflection.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4f.persona_text: Yeah. I was miserable. And I don't want to put my family through that again. They were relieved when I started smoking again, honestly. At least I was bearable to live with.

choice_points.option_text: So quitting created so much tension that your family actually seemed relieved when you went back to smoking. And now you're wondering if trying again would just repeat that painful experience for everyone.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection shows how the past experience creates fear about repeating it, and includes the family dimension. This is a key barrier they're facing.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Your family found it hard when you were quitting.

feedback.immediate: True but understated. They actually preferred them smoking, which is a powerful and painful message. The previous reflection captures that complexity better.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_4g.persona_text: Yeah. I can't win either way. Or maybe... maybe there is no winning. Maybe it's just about choosing which disappointment I can live with.

choice_points.option_text: You're shifting from looking for a perfect solution to thinking about which path feels more aligned with who you want to be.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection captures an important cognitive shift - from trying to avoid disappointment to choosing based on values. This is movement toward resolution.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: You're thinking about which disappointment you can accept.

feedback.immediate: Accurate but doesn't capture the subtle but important reframing they're doing - moving from 'can't win' to making an active choice. The previous option highlights this shift.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4h.persona_text: Right. I don't want to be defensive about it, but I also don't want to be judged for struggling with something this big.

choice_points.option_text: You want to be able to be honest about how difficult this is without that honesty being seen as making excuses or not caring enough.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This captures what they really need - to have their struggle validated rather than interpreted as lack of care or commitment. This is empathy at a deep level.
feedback.learning_note: Validation acknowledges the patient's experience without judgment.

choice_points.option_text: You want understanding, not judgment.

feedback.immediate: True but quite general. The previous option shows much deeper understanding of what specifically they need - to have their difficulty acknowledged without it being seen as lack of caring.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_4i.persona_text: Yes. Exactly. So when my daughter asks me to quit... I know she means well, but it feels like she's asking me to give up a piece of myself. And maybe I need to. Maybe that's what becoming a grandparent means. But it's hard.

choice_points.option_text: There's something about becoming a grandparent that might mean letting go of this part of yourself, even though that letting go is really hard and involves real loss.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection honors both sides - the possibility of growth/change and the reality of loss. This is sophisticated understanding of ambivalence.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

choice_points.option_text: Part of you thinks becoming a grandparent might mean it's time to quit, and part of you grieves what that would mean giving up.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This double-sided reflection captures both the emerging readiness and the grief. Both this and the previous option are excellent - they approach the complexity from slightly different angles.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5a.persona_text: That's it. I need both sides to have space. I think that's what I'm not getting from people - they want me to dismiss one side and embrace the other. But I can't do that yet.

choice_points.option_text: Both sides are real and important to you, and you need time and space to hold that tension rather than being pushed to resolve it prematurely.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This validates their need to sit with ambivalence rather than rush to resolution. This is respecting the contemplation process.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

node_5b.persona_text: Right. Which I guess tells me how deep this goes. If even my first grandchild isn't automatically making me quit... this is really complicated.

choice_points.option_text: You're recognizing just how significant smoking is in your life - that even something as powerful as becoming a grandparent doesn't automatically override that.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This reflects their growing insight about the depth of their relationship with smoking. This kind of self-awareness is valuable in contemplation.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5c.persona_text: It does. But I don't know if 'want' is strong enough to actually make me do it. You know?

choice_points.option_text: There's a difference between wanting something and feeling able to do it. The desire is there around your grandchild, but the confidence or readiness isn't quite there yet.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This complex reflection makes an important MI distinction - between importance (wanting to change) and confidence (believing you can). This helps clarify where they are.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5d.persona_text: Yes! You see it. This isn't about willpower or being weak. It's about relationships - the one I want to build with my grandchild and the ones I'm scared of losing with people who've been in my life for decades.

choice_points.option_text: At its heart, this is about connection. You're trying to figure out how to move toward one important connection without severing other important connections.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection identifies the core dilemma - how to build one relationship without losing others. This shows deep understanding of what makes this so difficult for them.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5e.persona_text: I am. I'm worried about losing a lot of things. But... I guess I'm also worried about what I'd lose if I don't quit. Missing out on time with my grandchild because I'm outside smoking, or worse, dying early and not being there for them.

choice_points.option_text: So you'd lose something either way. If you quit, you lose some relationships and parts of your routine. If you don't quit, you might lose time with your grandchild and potentially years of life.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This double-sided reflection frames both sides in terms of loss, which seems to be their framework. This helps them see the full picture of what's at stake.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5f.persona_text: Yeah. It's about who I am and who I want to be. And maybe those two people are different when it comes to smoking.

choice_points.option_text: You're noticing a gap between who you are now - someone whose life is intertwined with smoking - and who you want to be, especially as a grandparent.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection names the discrepancy between current and ideal self, which is a key motivator for change. You're amplifying their own insight.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5g.persona_text: It is right now. Maybe that will change when the baby's born. But right now, the fear is louder.

choice_points.option_text: For now, the fear has more volume, and you're open to the possibility that might shift when you meet your grandchild.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This honors where they are now (fear is stronger) while acknowledging their openness to change (might shift later). This respects their process.
feedback.learning_note: Continue practicing MI techniques to build your therapeutic skills.

node_5h.persona_text: Yeah. And I don't want to do that to them again. But I also think about my daughter, and how she'll feel when she sees me light up around her child. There's no good answer here.

choice_points.option_text: You're caught between not wanting to put your family through withdrawal again and not wanting to disappoint your daughter by smoking around your grandchild. Either path involves disappointing people you love.

feedback.immediate: Excellent! This double-sided reflection shows how both choices involve letting someone down. This validates why they feel stuck.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5i.persona_text: Yeah. I think that's it. It's not about finding the painless path. It's about deciding what matters most and accepting the costs that come with that.

choice_points.option_text: You're moving from looking for a way out of pain to thinking about which pain is worth it based on your values.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This complex reflection captures a profound shift in how they're thinking about their decision - from avoidance to values-based choice. This is real movement in contemplation.
feedback.learning_note: Reflections build rapport by showing you understand the patient's perspective.

node_5j.persona_text: Exactly. Thank you for that. I think I need to sit with all this, but it helps to feel like someone understands how hard this really is.

choice_points.option_text: You need to sit with the complexity a bit longer, and it helps to do that knowing someone sees how real and difficult this is for you.

feedback.immediate: Perfect! This honors their process - they need more time with the ambivalence, and they need that to be respected rather than rushed.
feedback.learning_note: Ambivalence is normal in change - our role is to explore it, not resolve it.

node_end_good.persona_text: This has been really helpful. I feel like I can think more clearly about this now. Not that I've decided anything, but I understand what I'm actually wrestling with better.

node_end_moderate.persona_text: Thanks for listening. I've got a lot to think about.
